titleEn: To God Belongs the Greatest Grace

verses.sadr: بَل كُلُّ سعِيكَ باطِلٌ إلَاّ التُّقَى
verses.ajuz: فإذا انقَضَى شيءٌ كأنْ لم يُفْعَلِ

verses.sadr: أوْ ذو زَوائِدَ لا يُطافُ بأرْضِهِ

verses.ajuz: وَلقد رَأى لُقمانُ أنْ لا يأتَلي

translationEn: To God belongs the greatest grace and honor, and to Him belongs all glory and the foundation of every compact. No one can erase His decree, nor is His judgment ever altered. He fashioned seven heavens above His throne, and set the earth firm below, with water and fire among His signs as admonition for those who reflect. All human striving is vain except piety, for when anything passes it is as though it never existed. Even the mightiest creatures of the wild and the legendary kings of old — Luqman, Tubba, Heraclius, Abraha, al-Harith — all were overcome by the nights of time, and the eloquent poets all walked the same road as Muraqqish and Muhalhal.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: primary wing feathers of a bird

context: A zuhd poem meditating on divine majesty and the transience of all earthly things, using the images of mighty kings and legendary creatures to illustrate that nothing survives the passage of time except God's decree and pious deeds.

titleEn: Convey My Message to Banu Kilab
translationEn: Carry this message if you encounter Banu Kilab and Amir, for events have their own guardians; deliver it too to Banu Numayr and the maternal kin of the slain among Banu Hilal: that the far-traveled envoy now rests settled at Tayman of the shades.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one who arrives, an envoy or messenger

context: A brief tribal message poem in which Labid directs a messenger to carry tidings to allied clans of Banu Amir, referencing a slain kinsman and reporting where the wandering traveler has finally settled.

titleEn: Rise When the Carefree Sleep, Lament Awf
translationEn: Rise when the carefree are asleep and lament Awf of generous deeds; Awf of the horsemen and the councils, of neighing steeds and swaying lances. O Awf, most forbearing of all the forbearing and most eloquent of all speakers — O Awf, you were our leader and the last of the earliest noble company.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the carefree one, one without sorrow

context: A short elegy in the truncated kamil meter in which Labid urges a mourning woman to rise at night and lament the fallen warrior Awf, praising him as the greatest horseman, speaker, and leader of his people.

titleEn: Ask Man What He Truly Seeks

verses.sadr: إذا المَرءُ أسْرَى لَيلَةً ظَنَّ أنَّهُ
verses.ajuz: قَضَى عَمَلًا والمَرْءُ ما عاشَ عامِلُ

verses.sadr: فَقُولا لَهُ إنْ كانَ يَقْسِمُ أمْرَهُ
فتَعْلمَ أنْ لا أنتَ مُدْرِكُ ما مضَى
verses.ajuz: وَلا أنتَ ممّا تَحذَرُ النّفسُ وَائِلُ

verses.sadr: فإنْ أنتَ لم تَصْدُقْكَ نَفسُكَ فانتسبْ
فإنْ لم تَجِدْ مِنْ دونِ عَدْنانَ باقيًا
أرَى النَّاسَ لَا يَدرُونَ ما قَدرُ أمرِهمْ
verses.ajuz: بلى: كلُّ ذي لُبٍّ إلى اللّهِ وَاسِلُ

verses.sadr: ألا كُلُّ شيءٍ ما خَلا اللّهُ باطِلُ
لهُ المُلْكُ في ضاحي مَعَدٍّ وأسلَمَتْ
إذا مَسَّ أسْآرَ الطُّيورِ صَفَتْ لَهُ

verses.ajuz: إذا ما انتَشَى لم تحْتَضِرْهُ العَوَاذِلُ

verses.sadr: على ما تُرِيهِ الخمرُ إذْ جاشَ بحْرُهُ
لَها حَجَلٌ قَد قَرَّعَتْ مِن رؤُوسِهِ
فإنَّ امرأً يَرْجُو الفَلاحَ وَقَد رَأى

verses.ajuz: ذُرَى أجَإٍ إذْ لاحَ فيها مُواسِلُ

verses.sadr: فبادُوا فَما أمسَى على الأرْضِ مِنهُمُ
كأنْ لم يكُنْ بالشِّرْعِ منهُمْ طلائِعٌ
verses.ajuz: فلَمْ تَرْعَ سَحًّا في الرَّبيعِ القَنابِلُ
ذِوَي الضِّمْرِ لمّا زالَ عَنها القبَائِلُ

translationEn: Do not ask what man is striving for — is it a debt to be repaid or mere delusion and vanity? His traps are laid along his very path, and he perishes when fate's snares miss him. Know: everything besides God is vain, and every pleasure must inevitably pass. All peoples will one day be touched by a small death that turns fingertips yellow. The poem then mourns al-Nu'man ibn al-Mundhir, the great Lakhmid king of al-Hira, celebrating his generosity with aged wine, his horses, his slave-girls, and the legendary armor of David — all swept away by time, as were the Ghassanids of Syria, leaving nothing behind but a story for inquirers.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a small fatal illness, little death
first pressing of wine, finest wine
the dignified chief, lord of his people
the beads of kingship, royal emblems

context: One of Labid's most celebrated wisdom poems, opening with the famous verse 'ألا كل شيء ما خلا الله باطل' — a cornerstone of Arabic moral poetry — and combining philosophical reflection on mortality with an elegy for the Lakhmid king al-Nu'man ibn al-Mundhir and his legendary court.

titleEn: Piety Is the Finest Gain

verses.ajuz: أوْ قرَا بي عَدْوُ جَوْنٍ قَد أبَلّ

verses.sadr: غيرَ أنْ لا تَكذبِنْها في التُّقَى

verses.ajuz: زَلَّ عَن ظَهرِ الصَّفا ماءُ الوَشَلْ

verses.sadr: لمْ أقِلْ إلَاّ عَلَيهِ أوْ عَلى

verses.ajuz: كُلَّ يومٍ تَبْتَلَي ما في الخِلَلْ
عِندَ ذي تاجٍ إذا قالَ فَعَلْ

translationEn: The poem opens declaring that piety toward God is the finest gain, and that all one's speed and patience are only by His leave. Praising God alone with no equal, the poet describes an arduous desert journey on a strong she-camel while offering practical wisdom: repay every favor, do not heed the whispers of laziness, discipline the soul, keep vigil through the night. He praises his own courage and his horse, and boasts of his tribe's victories in battle. The poem closes mourning Arbad, his lost brother, bitter to enemies but sweet as honey to kin.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: spoils of war; here: gain, bounty
bitter in taste; fierce to enemies

context: One of Labid's longest poems in al-Ramal meter, combining pious Islamic verses on God's omnipotence, praise of his she-camel, advice on diligence and piety, boasting of tribal glory in battle, and an elegy for his brother Arbad — reflecting Labid's life spanning both the Jahiliyya and Islam.

titleEn: We Come to You, Best of All Creation

verses.ajuz: وَقَد ذَهِلتْ أُمُّ الصَّبيِّ عنِ الطّفْلِ
منَ الجُوعِ صُمْتًا لا يُمِرُّ وَلا يُحلي

verses.sadr: وَلا شيءَ مِمّا يأكُلُ النّاسُ عِندَنَا

verses.ajuz: وَأينَ يَفِرُّ النَاسُ إلَاّ إلى الرُّسْلِ

verses.sadr: فإن تَدْعُ بالسّقْيا وبالعَفْوِ تُرْسلِ الـ
verses.ajuz: سماءُ لَنا والأمرُ يَبقى على الأصْلِ

translationEn: We come to you, O best of all creation, seeking your mercy from the hardship of drought and famine we have endured; we come while the virgin's chest bleeds with hunger and the mother has forgotten her infant in her distress. Even the brave man has fallen silent from starvation, tasting neither bitterness nor sweetness. We have nothing to eat but the wretched mixture of hair and blood that famine forces upon us. We have no refuge but you — for where else does humanity flee except to the prophets? If you pray for rain and forgiveness, the sky will send down its gift and matters will return to their proper order.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: nicknaming (the snake) to avoid evil omen
a type of snake; also a brave man
food made of blood and camel hair in famine

context: A petition poem addressed to the Prophet Muhammad, composed during the Year of Drought (Am al-Ramada), in which Labid and his tribe come seeking intercession, describing the extreme suffering of famine in vivid and moving imagery.

titleEn: Old Ruins of Khawla at al-Rusays

verses.sadr: قدْ قُدتُ في غَلَسِ الظلام، وطيرُهُ
غَرْبًا لَجُوجًا في العِنَانِ إذا انتحى

translationEn: The poem begins with the ruins of Khawla at al-Rusays, evoking the abandoned campsite whose earthen windbreak has crumbled. Labid turns to describe a powerful she-camel and a wild onager, painting them with masterful naturalistic detail as they roam the desert and water at a hidden spring. The poem then pivots to tribal boasting: declaring his lineage in the clan of Amir has shielded him from all injustice, and listing the great battle days of his people — day of Ruhraihan, day of al-Nukhail — where they stood firm when the alliances of Asad and Dhubyan and Tamim all faltered.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: ruins, traces of an abandoned campsite

context: A long fakhr qasida following the classical tripartite structure: nasib (ruins and beloved), rahil (she-camel and wild animal journey), and fakhr (tribal boasting), culminating in praise of Banu Amir's generosity, forbearance, and bravery in named historical battles.

titleEn: Deserted and Bare Are Wasit and Baram

verses.sadr: وَبِكُلِّ ذلكَ قَدْ سَعَيْتُ إلى العُلَى
وَلَقَدْ عَلِمْتِ لو انَّ عِلْمَكِ نافعٌ

translationEn: The familiar encampments at Wasit and Baram are emptied of their people, the valleys and watering places left bare. I remember when the whole tribe gathered there with gaming and drinking fellowship. Only horses roam free or stand tethered; thoroughbreds exercised in the cold hours by stable boys. I sheltered a desperate night-wanderer who arrived with nothing and fed him until the month of Haram brought abundance again. At a morning gathering I distributed feast-bowls piled high with camel humps among a company of thick-necked strongmen. Through all of this I have striven for glory; then to his wife the poet declares: you know well that I outdo my rivals in generosity and I protect my honor from every stain.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the ancient Arab gambling game with arrows
a man turned away from door to door, a stranger
the sound of dogs, the noise of a settled camp
black wooden bowls of the finest quality

context: A fakhr poem opening with a nasib on deserted encampments, then celebrating the poet's personal generosity — sheltering night wanderers, feeding assemblies — before closing with a reproach to his wife, asserting his unmatched bounty and honor.

titleEn: I Said to My Companions at Dhat Ghisl
translationEn: I said to my two companions at Dhat Ghisl: halt with me at this permanent grave, so we may see how its builders arched it over Hibban of noble lineage. We slew nine in vengeance for Abu Lubaina and made the freed clients equal to the pure-blooded kin.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: arched, raised the ceiling of (the tomb)
freed clients, allies of the clan

context: A brief elegy in which Labid pauses at a grave and directs his companions to contemplate it; he mentions taking blood revenge — slaying nine men — for the deceased, and elevating even the non-Arab clients to the status of full tribal members.

titleEn: The Encampments Are Erased, Their Halting Place and Their Abode

verses.sadr: بَلْ ما تَذَكَّرُ مِنْ نَوَارَ وقد نَأتْ

verses.ajuz: أن قد أحَمَّ مع الحُتُوفِ حِمَامُها

verses.sadr: بل أنتِ لا تدرين كم مِنْ ليلةٍ
حتى إذا ألْقَتْ يدًا في كافرٍ
إنّا إذا التقتِ المجَامِعُ لم يَزَلْ
فَضْلًا، وذو كَرِمٍ يُعينُ على النَّدى

verses.ajuz: إذ لا يميلُ معَ الهَوى أحلامُها

translationEn: Labid's Mu'allaqa opens with desolate ruins at Mina where time has erased all traces of habitation, sacred months and profane months having passed alike. Spring rains have renewed the land and wild animals have settled — gazelles, ostriches, wild cows — around the ruins now inscribed by floods like ancient manuscripts. He turns to remember Nawar, his distant beloved from the Murra tribe, then describes a camel and a wild ass with extraordinary naturalistic vividness. The poem's central section recounts a night of drinking, generosity, and battle vigil, and closes with extensive tribal boasting: his clan of Amir divides spoils justly, shelters neighbors and widows in winter, and stands firm when all other alliances falter — for God assigned them the greatest portion of honor.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a woman who tattoos; her designs compared to ruins
partners in the maysir gambling game
an inherited way of life, established tradition

context: Labid's Mu'allaqa — one of the Seven Hanging Odes — is perhaps the supreme achievement of pre-Islamic Arabic poetry, renowned for its stunning descriptions of rain-renewed ruins, a powerful camel, a hunted wild ass, a fearful wild cow, a night of drinking, and extended tribal boasting that culminates in a meditation on divine providence and accepted fate.

titleEn: For Hind at the Heights of al-Agarr Are Ruins

verses.ajuz: لها بَعْدَ كأسٍ في العظامِ هَمِيمُ

verses.sadr: فلَم تَرْضَ ضَحْلَ الماءِ حتّى تَمَهَّرَتْ
شَفى النَّفْسَ ما خُبِّرتُ مَرَّانُ أزْهفَتْ

translationEn: Ruins belonging to Hind stretch from the heights of al-Agarr to Uhud, etched like tattoos. The poet crosses barren wastes smooth as a shield-back on a powerful she-camel whose saddle creaks like a drunkard singing with Babylonian wine in his bones. She is driven to a wooded watering place by the al-Qurnatayn until she drinks her fill and emerges adorned with duckweed like a necklace. Then the poem boasts of the tribe's victory at the day of al-Nukhail, where the clans of Ju'fi ibn Sa'd were struck down as if poisoned, and saved only by a fighting band of the clan of Ka'b — yet all this, says Labid, is matched by what we have done, without claiming arrogance.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the camel's wooden saddle-pole that creaks
strong wine that makes one shiver
duckweed, the green algae on still water
a hard-fought battle, a day of fierce combat

context: A fakhr poem beginning with nasib ruins of Hind, then a vivid camel journey passage, before pivoting to boasting of the tribe's fighting record, particularly the day of al-Nukhail against the clans of Ju'fi, closing with the dignified understatement that deeds answer deeds.

titleEn: She Saw That I Had Grown Pale and Lean

verses.sadr: إذا ما دَرُّهَا لم يَقْرِ ضيفًا
وكَم فينا إذا ما المحلُ أبْدى

translationEn: A woman saw that the poet had grown pale and wasted, worn by the pursuit of distant concerns and great anxieties. He catalogues what he has endured: resisting arrogant adversaries without rancor, shielding a client from injustice, crossing vast wildernesses on lean she-camels that startled sleeping sandgrouse. He swears by her father: no tribe compares to his own for the guest who settles among them or the destitute, for in drought they slaughter the fattest hump-backed camels rather than spare the young — and when ancient lineage is reckoned, his clan's glory, rank, and deep-rooted nobility are found unmatched.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: tributes, shares of meat accorded to chiefs

context: A fakhr poem in al-Wafir meter opening with the poet's haggard appearance as seen by a woman, then cataloguing hardships endured and service rendered to clients, before culminating in a proud declaration of unmatched tribal generosity and ancient nobility.

titleEn: Foolishly You Blamed Me, Speaking Without Blame

verses.sadr: لو كان حيٌّ في الحياةِ مُخَلَّدًا

translationEn: The poem opens with a sharp rebuke to his wife: her reproach is foolishness and her weeping unworthy. Let the mighty dead — Iram, Himyar, the two Harriths, the Tubba' kings, Alexander of the two horns, and even David who forged iron mail — teach her that no living being is immortal. The fates stripped David of his finest craft as if in an unavoidable ambush. So leave off blaming; a generous man is not governed by the blamer's tongue. Labid then recounts his deeds: deflecting a great calamity, relieving a wounded man's distress in battle, then a fine passage describing an arduous desert crossing on a strong she-camel, followed by description of a wild bull oryx grazing in spring pastures.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one who has committed a blameworthy act
a horse with a hard, rounded head

context: A fakhr poem rebuking the poet's wife for her blame, using the mortality of legendary rulers and prophets to silence her complaints, then pivoting to self-praise through battle deeds and a vivid camel-journey passage ending with a description of a wild bull in spring pasture.

titleEn: When News of Tufayl and His Clan Reached Me
translationEn: When news of Tufayl and his kin reached me at nightfall, a burning anger settled in my chest. He threw with his left hand at a paradise-bred she-camel of al-Abqari stock, with striped necks and dappled forelegs — a sword drawn from the white blades after every scabbard had cracked from the hilt. Tight-girdled, kohl-lining his eyes at midnight on his journey, he emerges bright-faced in the morning without a trace of despair.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: burning thirst; here: a burning anger or grief
of al-Abqari, mythical land; excellent, peerless
the leading feathers; here: the forelegs of a she-camel
the sword-tang, the part inserted into the hilt

context: A short fakhr fragment in which news of a rival's action provokes anger; Labid describes either the she-camel involved or an admirable night-traveler with kohl-lined eyes, arriving in the morning undaunted — a brief but richly detailed vignette of tribal honor and nocturnal valor.

titleEn: Our Land Wept When We Departed
translationEn: Our land wept when we departed, and the valleys of Sufaira and al-Ghayam bade us farewell. The tribal encampment that rang with life in the evening now lies empty of any soul. We disdained to let the Suda' and Nahd clans settle there after the sacred month ended. Had they found the clan of Banu Jariyy and Taym al-Lat, the young she-camels would have been scattered in panic by swift thoroughbreds with bits that blunt the edges of their mature horses' teeth, and by every flexible straight lance and sharp sword whose striking edges are coated with deadly poison — while by the side of Suwayqa the massed livestock crush the tamarisk spears.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: we disdained, were too proud to allow

context: A wasf poem recounting the tribe's departure from their ancestral land, expressing pride that they would not allow rival clans to settle in their place, and celebrating their military might with descriptions of swift horses, flexible lances, and poisoned swords.

titleAr: عفَا الرَّسْمُ أمْ لا، بَعْدَ حَوْلٍ تَجَرَّمَا
titleEn: Erased or Not, a Full Year Has Passed

verses.sadr: عفَا الرَّسْمُ أمْ لا، بَعْدَ حَوْلٍ تَجَرَّمَا

verses.ajuz: ولم نَخْشَ مِنْ أسْبابِهَا أنْ تَجَذَّمَا

verses.sadr: فَدَعْ ذا وَبَلِّغْ قَوْمَنَا إنْ لَقِيتَهُمْ
verses.ajuz: وهل يُخْطِئنَ اللومُ منْ كانَ ألْوَما
من المُنْحَنَى مِنْ عَاقِلٍ ثمَّ خَيَّمَا
وحَلَّ الضِّبابُ في عليٍّ بنِ أسْلَما
على النأْيِ إلَاّ أنْ يُحَيَّا وَيَسْلَمَا

verses.sadr: وقُل لابنِ عمرٍو ما ترى رَأْيَ قَوْمِكمْ

verses.ajuz: صَليبٌ إذا مَا الدهرُ أجشَم مُعظِمَا
حُصَيْنُ بنُ عَوْفٍ بعدما كانَ أشْأَما

verses.sadr: وفكَّ أبَا الجَوَّابِ عمرُو بنُ خالدٍ
verses.ajuz: وما كانَ عَنْهُ ناكِلًا حيثُ يَمَّمَا
إلى فاتكٍ ذي جُرْأةٍ قَدْ تَحَتَّمَا

verses.sadr: وإنْ لم يَكنْ إلا القِتَالُ فإنَّنَا
أبى خَسْفَنَا أنْ لا تَزَالَ رُوَاتُنَا

verses.ajuz: شُجَاعٌ إذا ما آنسَ السِّرْبَ ألْجَمَا
إلى كُلِّ مَحبوك من السَّرْو أيْهَمَا
أبا الحصن إذْ عافَ الشَّرَابَ وأقْسَمَا

verses.sadr: فأبْلِغْ بَني بكرٍ إذا مَا لَقيتَهَا
verses.ajuz: عَلى خَيرَ ما يُلْقَى به مَنْ تَزَغَّمَا
ولم يُبْقِ هذا الدهرُ في العَيْش مَنْدَمَا

translationEn: Whether the ruins of Asma' have been erased after a year or not, they remain like a silent manuscript. The poem moves to address tribal allies — sending a message to the clan confederates of Amr ibn Amir — and narrates the drifting apart of allied clans and each choosing their own territories. When left to ourselves, declares Labid, we chose the noblest course: waiting, deliberating, relying on our deep-rooted tribal strength. He lists specific acts of military valor — freeing a captive, meeting the clans of al-Harithain in battle — and closes by addressing Banu Bakr: our common father binds us; accept the honorable course, for death holds no harm for those who deserve honor.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: was completed, came to an end (of a year)
a deeply-rooted tribe, an ancient foundation
a vanguard raiding party, a strike force
the rope by which a horse is led; controllability
a cause for regret, something to be regretted

context: A long fakhr poem in al-Tawil meter that weaves nasib (ruins of Asma'), a narrative of tribal alliance and separation, specific battle boasts including named days and individuals, and a diplomatic message to Banu Bakr offering honorable peace or war, closing with a solitary two-word fragment.

titleEn: When Amir Called Me to Revile Them

verses.ajuz: أبَيْتُ وَإنْ كان ابنُ عَيْساءَ ظَالمَا

verses.sadr: بَلَى: أيُّنَا ما كانَ شرًّا لمالكٍ
verses.ajuz: فَلا زالَ في الدُّنيا مَلُومًا ولائِمَا

translationEn: When Amir called upon me to revile them, I refused — even though Ibn Aysa' had wronged me — for that would have meant making al-Sandari my equal and treating whole clans as if they were distant strangers, and digging up from their graves noble ancestors who tied my amulets, on whose shoulders and in whose laps I played as a child, who called me Mufid and Asim. Instead: let whichever of us has been worse to Malik remain forever blamed and a blamer in this world.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: an equal, a peer, a match
amulets tied on children for protection
to exhume, to dig up from graves

context: A hikma poem of tribal loyalty and self-restraint: Labid refuses to engage in the dishonor of reviling kin even when urged, because his antagonists are the same elders who raised him and tied his childhood amulets — a powerful statement of the sacred bonds that override personal grievance.

titleEn: Gone Are the Guardian and the Defender

verses.ajuz: إذا لم يُرْجَ رِسْلٌ في السَّوَامِ

verses.sadr: وإنَّا قَدْ يُرَى ما نَحْنُ فيه

translationEn: Gone are the guardian and the defender, the one who shielded us from injustice in the day of dispute. The day they divided Arbad's inheritance by lots confirmed separation forever. Arbad, the horseman of battle whenever the warriors planted their stakes in the earth — his she-camels fly as shares to partners, in pairs and singly, while leadership passes to the young heir. Labid praises Arbad's boundless generosity to the poor, the orphan, and the neighbor, his matchless company at wine-drinking, and his fierce bravery in battle where his decisive stroke left a chief face-down and bleeding. He says farewell: Arbad, you were our imam and our thread of coherence — without you, people are mere echoes and hollow skulls, charmed by food and drink as ancient 'Ad and Iram were charmed before their destruction.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the one who preserves and protects
equal shares of inheritance among co-heirs
one stripped of his possessions, a destitute man
thread that holds beads together; order, coherence
skulls; in poetry, souls of the dead become owls

context: Labid's celebrated elegy for his brother Arbad ibn Rabi'ah, who was killed by lightning shortly after the poet's encounter with the Prophet; the poem mourns Arbad as the incomparable guardian, host, companion, warrior, and source of all tribal cohesion, ending with an image of humanity bewitched like the destroyed peoples of ancient legend.

titleEn: The Ruins at Muta'li' and Aban Have Faded

verses.sadr: خَلَدَتْ ولم يَخْلُدْ بها مَنْ حَلَّها
لَوْ كانَ يَزْجُرُها لَقَدْ سَنَحتْ له

translationEn: The ruins at Muta'li' and Aban have faded and grown ancient at al-Hubs and al-Suban; they stretch on to the highland pools of Sara and al-Qanan, resembling Yemeni script traced by a practiced hand on palm ribs. The ancient campsite of the Hanzali woman has endured while those who once lived there have not, replaced now by wild she-cows with their calves and gazelles with their young. Labid turns from the ruins to follow his she-camel to an ancient well at dawn, waters her, then mounts up and his camel carries him like an Indian ship riding its warped planks across the sand. The poem's finest passage describes a solitary wild donkey standing in the predawn then racing across the desert — confronting the hunter's hounds with its horn and thrashing them aside — followed by a description of a tall ostrich picking sparse desert plants and returning at dusk to its clutch of early-hatched chicks.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: hunting hounds set loose on prey
a wild animal's horn used for goring
early-born offspring, chicks hatched in spring

context: A long descriptive qasida in al-Kamil meter: after the nasib over ruins, Labid describes a dawn watering at an ancient desert well, then delivers twin animal poems — first the wild onager facing hounds and escaping, then the ostrich tending its nest — achieving some of the most exquisitely detailed nature poetry in the Arabic tradition.

titleEn: I Visited the Tribe's Abodes at al-Sab'an

verses.sadr: وإنِّي لأعطِي المالَ مَنْ لا أوَدُّهُ
وَذِي لُطُفٍ لو كانَ يَعْلَمُ أنَّهُ

translationEn: I visited the tribe's dwelling at al-Sab'an — my eyes racing ahead like the full moon — abodes once sheltered by fair-cheeked women like the wild cows of the open plain, young and middle-aged. Yet I give wealth even to those I do not love, and clothe men I despise. There is one who secretly inquires about me, wishing I had drunk poison in my own saliva; and another who fawns over me with such subtlety that if he knew his own blood were my cure, he would bleed himself for me.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I visited, I went over to
wild cows of the open plain (image of beautiful women)
a young woman reaching puberty; a first-calving cow

context: A short wasf poem opening with a visit to abandoned ruins of a tribe, evoking the beauty of those who once lived there, then pivoting to sharp social observations: the poet gives generously even to enemies, while others secretly wish him dead or play at friendship for self-interest.

titleEn: If Your People Have Wronged, O Yazid
translationEn: If your people have erred, O Yazid, then I lament to you Ja'far and the Solitary. I will not forgive the clan of Baghid their foolishness and their slanderous tongues; I will take satisfaction from their nobles for my honor, for they are neither faithful to their word nor close kin to me. For the remnant of true lineage is ours, and the bearers of blood-debts and the lancers in battle — deep-rooted clans who have defended the white highlands of Najd, while you are counted among the lowly hangers-on.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I announce the death of, I lament
the nobles, the chiefs of a clan
the bearing of blood-money responsibilities for others

context: A brief fakhr fragment in which Labid rebukes an addressee called Yazid and declares he will not forgive the insults of the Banu Baghid clan, asserting his own tribe's deep-rooted nobility and blood-feud obligations against men who are neither trustworthy nor truly noble.

titleEn: I Hear That Abu Hanif Has Blamed Me
translationEn: Transmitted with a prose editorial note explaining that Labid composed this poem on the night of his death, instructing his daughter to prepare two feasting bowls for the mosque and announce his funeral. The poem proper opens: I hear that Abu Hanif has numbered himself among my blamers. My dear son, have you seen my uncles, the sons of Umm al-Banin? My father was the one to whom widows in winter flocked as his household dependents. He addresses his son on the duties of generous hospitality: be chaste in your dealings with female neighbors but give them from your fattest slaughtered beast; offer the topmost hump of the pot, for cauldrons are like she-camels most productive when well-grazed. When you bury your father, lay wood and clay and solid stone slabs over him — for all the stone will not protect his face from the soil. Then take your lesson from your kinsmen's eulogy at his graveside, as they return empty-handed from the watch. These are true honors: preserve them and you shall never be a loser. The poem ends with the soul speaking to the dying poet: she complains he has carried her seventy years already — if he lives three more he will reach eighty.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: those who seek, avengers seeking blood
your fattest slaughtered animal (from the maysir game)
the topmost best piece in the cooking pot
fertilized, productive (said of camels and here cauldrons)
solid dense stone slabs for grave covering
the foetal-position grave, the man curled in burial
a loser, one who has been cheated or diminished

context: According to tradition, Labid's deathbed poem — composed on the night he died at the extreme old age of perhaps 140 years — combining instructions to his son on generous hospitality and proper burial customs with a meditation on noble reputation and a final conversation with his own soul as it complains of its long burden.
